description: This figure illustrates the effect of Al content (3 to 10 wt.%) on mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with two carbon levels (0.85 wt.% C: dashed lines; 0.95 wt.% C: solid lines), all processed via water quenching from 1150 °C followed by aging at 550 °C for 16 h. Three groups of properties are plotted: the top panel shows ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangular markers) and 0.2% yield strength (0.2%YS, hollow triangular markers, unit: MPa); the middle panel shows ductility (reduction of area ψ, hollow circular markers; elongation δ, solid circular markers, unit: %); the bottom panel shows U-notch impact toughness (Kcu, rhombus markers, unit: J/cm²). Key trends: For Al < ~6 wt.%, strength and ductility stay nearly constant while impact toughness increases. When Al exceeds ~6.2 wt.%, UTS and YS rise significantly (to >1200 MPa at 10 wt.% Al for 0.95 wt.% C) alongside gradual ductility reduction. Impact toughness peaks at 6-7 wt.% Al (max ~180 J/cm² for 0.85 wt.% C) then drops sharply at higher Al, while higher C content improves strength but reduces ductility and toughness. This figure supports that Al content controls κ-phase precipitation hardening during aging, tuning mechanical property balance for structural Fe-Mn-Al-C steel applications.

This optical micrograph shows the microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel with 4.6 wt.% Al after aging at 600 °C for 16 h, with a scale bar of 20 μm. Polygonal austenite grains with clear grain boundaries are observed, with fine dispersed κ-phase precipitates distributed intragranularly, and partial grains contain annealing twin structures. The low aluminum content of this sample leads to carbide precipitation tendency along grain boundaries, which is reported to form a continuous network that severely degrades impact toughness. This figure directly visualizes the second phase precipitation behavior during aging of low-Al Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, provides microstructural evidence for the composition-dependent embrittlement mechanism, and supports the core conclusion that composition should be optimized to avoid harmful grain boundary precipitates to meet mechanical property requirements for structural applications.

This figure presents two optical micrographs characterizing δ-ferrite microstructure in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel with 10.5 wt.% Al, both equipped with a 30 μm scale bar. The first subgraph corresponds to the as-cast state, showing lathy, worm-like continuous δ-ferrite phases distributed in the austenite matrix. The second subgraph corresponds to the post-deformation state, exhibiting fractured, discrete δ-ferrite phases with polygonal morphology, mostly distributed along austenite grain boundaries. As documented in the study, δ-ferrite forms in this alloy system when Al content exceeds 10 wt.%, which reduces the technological plasticity and enhances mechanical property anisotropy of the alloy. The observed morphological evolution of δ-ferrite after deformation verifies that thermomechanical processing can adjust the distribution and morphology of δ-ferrite, supporting the composition optimization framework for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels to eliminate embrittling phases and meet mechanical property requirements for structural applications.

This group of optical micrographs shows the microstructures of Fe-Mn-Al-C system austenitic steels fabricated via jet spraying rapid solidification, hot pressing at 1150 °C, and quenching from 1100 °C, with a uniform scale bar of 30 μm for both subgraphs. Subgraph (a) presents the microstructure of the sample with 9.8 wt% Al, where no δ-ferrite is observed, consisting of austenite grains with acicular and strip-like morphologies. Subgraph (b) shows the sample with 14 wt% Al, where polygonal austenite grains are dominant, and around 10 vol.% δ-ferrite (marked with an arrow) is detected at grain boundary regions. The comparison verifies that rapid solidification can suppress δ-ferrite formation when Al content is no higher than 12 wt%, while excess Al (14 wt%) leads to δ-ferrite precipitation, which supports the composition optimization strategy to avoid embrittling phases for good mechanical properties of the steel.

This figure plots saturation magnetization (4πJₛ, unit: G) as a function of carbon content (range: 0.8 to 1.06 wt%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with fixed 9 wt% Al and 30 wt% Mn, aged under different conditions to evaluate austenite aging intensity. Curves correspond to samples aged at temperatures from 350 °C to 550 °C for either 16 h (dashed lines) or 100 h (solid lines). Key trends: saturation magnetization rises with increasing C content for all aging conditions above 350 °C, as higher C content promotes precipitation of magnetic κ-phase during aging. For equivalent aging duration, magnetization first increases with aging temperature up to 400–450 °C, then decreases at 500–550 °C; longer aging time yields higher magnetization at the same temperature above 350 °C, indicating more extensive κ-phase precipitation. Samples aged at 350 °C show negligible magnetization irrespective of duration, confirming minimal aging occurs at this temperature. This figure supports the conclusion that carbon is the leading diffusion-mobile element driving the onset of austenite aging in this alloy system, with saturation magnetization serving as a qualitative proxy for aging process intensity.

This plot illustrates the effect of carbon content (0.3 to 1.1 wt.%) on mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with fixed 9 wt.% Al and 30 wt.% Mn, comparing two heat treatment conditions: water quenching from 1050 °C (dashed curves) and water quenching from 1050 °C followed by aging at 550 °C for 16 h (solid curves). Tested properties include ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and 0.2% yield strength (0.2%YS) (upper y-axis, unit: MPa), elongation (δ) and reduction of area (ψ) (middle y-axis, unit: %), and U-notch impact toughness (Kcu) (lower y-axis, unit: J/cm²). UTS and 0.2%YS increase monotonically with rising C content, with aged samples showing higher strength than as-quenched samples. Ductility (δ, ψ) and impact toughness Kcu first increase then decrease with C content, peaking at ~0.7 wt.% C; Kcu drops sharply when C content exceeds 1 wt.% due to grain boundary carbide precipitation. This figure supports that the optimized C content range to balance strength and toughness for structural applications is 0.7~1.0 wt.%.

This figure presents the dependence of mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with fixed 9 wt% Al and 0.9 wt% C, processed by water quenching from 1050 °C followed by aging at 550 °C for 16 h, on Mn content ranging from 24 wt% to 34 wt%. Three categories of performance are plotted: 1) Mechanical strength: ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, hollow triangles) both peak at ~1200 MPa and ~1020 MPa respectively in the Mn range of 26-29 wt%, then decrease gradually as Mn rises, dropping to ~950 MPa and ~880 MPa at 34 wt% Mn. 2) Ductility: reduction of area (ψ, hollow circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles) reach their maximum values of ~60% and ~44% around 30 wt% Mn, then drop sharply when Mn exceeds 32 wt%, to ~22% and ~11% at 34 wt% Mn respectively. 3) U-notch impact toughness (K_CU, hollow rhombuses) maintains at 100-115 J/cm² when Mn is 24-32 wt%, then plummets to ~30 J/cm² at 34 wt% Mn. The core conclusion is that the optimum combination of strength, ductility and toughness is achieved at Mn content of 28.5-30.5 wt%, while Mn content over 32 wt% causes severe embrittlement, supporting the composition optimization of this alloy system for structural applications.
